text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY -- SURVEILLANCE FOLLOW-UP NOTE
2-Year Survivorship Visit

ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient, surveillance

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Chen is a 54-year-old Taiwanese-American female, never-smoker, presenting for her 2-year post-treatment surveillance visit and formal survivorship assessment. She carries a diagnosis of HPV-positive (p16+) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, stage III (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition), treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT. She completed treatment approximately 24 months ago and has maintained a complete response on all subsequent imaging.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen was originally diagnosed at age 52 with a 2.8 cm left base of tongue squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-16 positive by RNA expression and p16 IHC, with a single ipsilateral left level IIA nodal metastasis (2.5 cm). NGS confirmed PIK3CA E545K mutation, TRAF3 loss-of-function, FAT1 alteration, and NOTCH1 VUS; TMB 5.2 muts/Mb; MSI-stable; PD-L1 CPS 65. She was treated with definitive IMRT (70 Gy in 35 fractions, SIB technique) with concurrent weekly cisplatin (40 mg/m2 x 6 of 7 planned cycles; one cycle held for transient nephrotoxicity; cumulative cisplatin dose approximately 390 mg/m2). She had a prophylactic PEG placed prior to treatment. Her acute course was complicated by grade 3 mucositis and dysphagia requiring full PEG dependence from week 5, a brief hospitalization post-treatment for pain management and rehydration, and grade 1 ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity. She achieved a complete metabolic and radiographic response on PET-CT and MRI neck at 12 weeks post-treatment. PEG was removed at approximately 5 months post-treatment after oral intake recovered to >85% of caloric needs. Surveillance imaging at 12, 18, and 24 months has been negative for locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease.

Today she returns for her 2-year survivorship visit. She reports feeling "essentially back to normal" and has returned to her full-time position as a high school principal. She denies any new neck masses, throat pain, odynophagia, hemoptysis, or constitutional symptoms. She has noted stable mild xerostomia and mild residual taste alteration, both of which she characterizes as manageable. No new swallowing difficulties -- tolerating a regular diet with thin liquids without coughing or choking episodes. No ear pain, hearing changes, or new neurologic symptoms. Fatigue fully resolved. No trismus symptoms. She was seen by endocrinology for radiation-induced hypothyroidism and her thyroid replacement is well-controlled on current dose of levothyroxine.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Head/neck: No new throat pain, no odynophagia, no neck mass, no otalgia, no epistaxis
- Respiratory: No dyspnea, no hemoptysis, no cough
- GI: No dysphagia on current diet; no nausea, vomiting, or weight loss; mild residual xerostomia noted; bowel habits normal on current levothyroxine dose
- Endocrine: Fatigue improved; no cold intolerance, no constipation (previously present with hypothyroidism); energy levels good
- Musculoskeletal: No jaw pain, no limited mouth opening
- Neuro: No focal neurologic symptoms
- Constitutional: No fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss
- Auditory: No new hearing complaints; aware of high-frequency deficit per prior audiogram
- Psychiatric/psychosocial: Good mood; actively participating in survivorship support group

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital signs: BP 118/74, HR 72, weight 61.1 kg (up from 60.2 kg at 6-month visit; essentially at pre-treatment baseline of 62 kg), BMI 22.7, ECOG PS 0.

General: Well-appearing Asian female in no acute distress; appears well-nourished and appropriately dressed.

Head: No scalp lesions. Temporal-mandibular joint: Full opening appreciated (41 mm, within 1 mm of pre-treatment baseline of 42 mm). No trismus.

Oral cavity/oropharynx: Mucosa intact; mild radiation-related mucosal pallor and reduced salivary pooling consistent with grade 1 xerostomia; tongue freely mobile; no mucosal ulceration, mass, or erythematous lesion suspicious for recurrence; pharyngeal walls with mild post-radiation atrophic changes; tonsils absent (radiation field).

Flexible nasopharyngoscopy: Performed at bedside. Nasopharynx clean. Oropharynx: base of tongue mucosa slightly irregular and mildly atrophic consistent with expected post-radiation change; no discrete mass, ulceration, or friable tissue identified; no mucosal lesion concerning for recurrence. Hypopharynx and supraglottis: Normal. Larynx: Vocal cords symmetric and fully mobile. No subglottic lesion.

Neck: No palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical chains bilaterally. Post-radiation fibrotic soft tissue change in left anterior cervical region, stable. A soft bruit is audible on auscultation over the bilateral common carotid arteries (mild), unchanged from prior visits. No tracheal deviation.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender. PEG site well-healed without keloid formation.

Skin: Mild hyperpigmentation of bilateral anterior neck consistent with radiation field (cosmetically noticeable but not clinically significant); no moist desquamation.

---

LAB RESULTS:
TSH: 1.7 mIU/L (normal range 0.4-4.0) -- well-controlled on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day.
Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal).
CBC: WBC 6.1 x10^9/L, Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Plt 218 x10^9/L -- within normal limits.
CMP: Creatinine 0.72 mg/dL (back to baseline, CrCl ~93 mL/min); electrolytes, LFTs within normal limits.
Magnesium: 2.0 mEq/L (normal; previously required supplementation post-treatment).

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
MRI Neck with contrast (2-year surveillance): No evidence of locoregional recurrence. Post-treatment mucosal changes at the left base of tongue are stable relative to the 12-month MRI. No cervical lymphadenopathy. The small left level IIA soft tissue residual noted on earlier scans has completely resolved and is no longer detectable. Radiation-related soft tissue changes in the left neck are stable. No radiation necrosis of the mandible or soft tissues. Carotid arteries with stable mild atherosclerotic changes without interval progression. Overall impression consistent with durable complete response at 24 months post-treatment.

Chest CT (last obtained at 18 months post-treatment): Negative for pulmonary nodules or thoracic metastatic disease. No new chest imaging obtained at this visit; will repeat at 30 months per protocol.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma, left base of tongue, stage III (T2N1M0) -- NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE (NED) at 24 months post-treatment.
   - Ms. Chen has achieved and maintained a complete response to definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT. This is entirely consistent with the excellent prognosis expected for HPV-positive oropharyngeal carcinoma in a never-smoking patient with favorable staging. At 2 years post-treatment with durable CMR on all imaging, her likelihood of locoregional recurrence is extremely low. The predominant remaining risk over the next 3-5 years is distant recurrence (lungs most common), though this too is low given her stage and biology.
   - Surveillance schedule transition: Per NCCN guidelines for NED patients at 2 years, will transition to every 6-month clinic visits for years 3-5, then annually thereafter. Annual neck MRI to continue. Chest CT at 30 months. TSH every 6 months ongoing.
   - Next clinic visit in 6 months.

2. Late radiation toxicity profile -- overall excellent.
   a. Xerostomia grade 1: Mildly symptomatic, well-managed. Continuing pilocarpine 5 mg TID; patient tolerating well. Encouraged continued excellent oral hydration and dental hygiene. Will maintain prescription. No dose escalation needed at this time.
   b. Dysphagia: Functionally resolved. Patient tolerating regular diet with thin liquids. The mild pharyngeal dysmotility and trace aspiration on thin liquids documented at 5 months on MBS has improved clinically; formal re-evaluation is not felt necessary today given asymptomatic status, but if symptoms recur, will repeat modified barium swallow.
   c. Trismus: Resolved. Mouth opening 41 mm (41 mm today vs. pre-treatment 42 mm). Jaw exercises can be continued as home maintenance.
   d. Ototoxicity: Grade 1 bilateral high-frequency hearing loss (4 kHz threshold shift), stable. No hearing aids required. Last audiogram at 18 months showed no progression. Will repeat at 3-year mark.
   e. Taste: Partial recovery of taste function (patient estimates ~75% normal, improved from approximately 60% at 12 months). Dysgeusia grade 1, improving. Expected to continue gradual recovery over years 2-5.
   f. Carotid atherosclerosis: Mild bilateral intimal-medial thickening without significant stenosis on carotid duplex at 14 months. Soft bruit on exam. Annual duplex surveillance per vascular surgery recommendation ongoing; next duplex at 24-month mark from initial study (i.e., approximately 26 months post-treatment). Patient counseled on vascular risk reduction: optimize BP, avoid smoking (never-smoker -- good), maintain active lifestyle.
   g. No osteoradionecrosis: Dental health maintained; on fluoride supplementation. No high-risk dental procedures planned; patient reminded to always notify treating dentist of prior radiation to jaw before any extractions or implants.

3. Radiation-induced hypothyroidism: Managed in collaboration with endocrinology. Currently on levothyroxine 100 mcg/day. TSH 1.7 mIU/L today -- optimally controlled. Continue current dose; TSH recheck in 6 months at next clinic visit (or sooner if symptoms recur). Patient aware of symptoms of both under- and over-replacement.

4. Survivorship and psychosocial: Patient reports excellent quality of life, return to full-time work, and active participation in a head and neck cancer survivorship support group. Referred to survivorship clinic at conclusion of today's visit for formal 2-year survivorship care plan documentation. Discussed ongoing importance of sun protection (head/neck), dental care, cardiovascular risk monitoring, and avoidance of exposure to tobacco even as a never-smoker given occupational considerations. Patient's adolescent children's HPV vaccination status discussed again; she confirmed all children have completed the series.

5. Oncologist's perspective on treatment intensity: Retrospectively, given this patient's outstanding outcome -- durable CMR at 2 years, acceptable late toxicity profile, return to full function -- she represents an ideal candidate for whom a de-escalation protocol (reduced cisplatin dose or reduced radiation dose such as 60 Gy) might have achieved equivalent oncologic control with potentially less xerostomia and ototoxicity. This observation is documented for institutional research purposes (prospective de-escalation data collection). No clinical action is taken on this point; it is noted for educational and research context only.

Patient verbalized understanding of today's discussion. All questions answered. She is seen and discussed with the attending physician.

Return to clinic in 6 months.

Electronically signed,
Head and Neck Medical Oncology
